title: Chlamydial Infections
section: Diseases Caused by Rickettsiae Mycoplasmas and Chlamydiae
authors: Matthew M. Hamill, Thomas C. Quinn

items.title: Classification and Clinical Relevance of Chlamydia Species
items.content: Chlamydiae are obligate intracellular bacteria classified into several species, with C. trachomatis being notable for causing ocular and urogenital infections. The clinical relevance lies in the differentiation between biovars, particularly when LGV is suspected, as it requires a longer treatment duration.
keyPoints: Chlamydiae are obligate intracellular bacteria.
C. trachomatis has two biovars: trachoma and LGV.
Trachoma causes ocular infections leading to preventable blindness; urogenital infections are sexually transmitted or neonatally transmitted.
18 serovars of C. trachomatis fall into three groups: trachoma (A, B, Ba, C), oculogenital (D–K), and LGV ($ L_{1} $–$ L_{3} $).
Serovar identification is generally not clinically important except for suspected LGV cases, which require longer treatment.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.title: Diagnostic and Biological Characteristics of Chlamydiae
items.content: Chlamydiae produce characteristic intracytoplasmic inclusions visible by specific staining methods. They are nonmotile, gram-negative bacteria with a unique cell wall structure lacking peptidoglycan, relying on disulfide bonds for structural integrity.
keyPoints: Chlamydiae replicate within host cells forming membrane-bound inclusions.
Intracytoplasmic inclusions can be visualized by direct fluorescent antibody or Giemsa staining.
Chlamydiae are nonmotile, gram-negative, obligate intracellular bacteria.
They possess RNA and DNA but lack peptidoglycan; structural integrity depends on disulfide binding of outer-membrane proteins.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.title: Chlamydiae Growth Cycle: Understanding Elementary and Reticulate Bodies
items.content: The chlamydial growth cycle involves two morphologic forms: the infectious elementary body (0.25–0.35 µm) and the replicating reticulate body (0.5–1 µm). The process begins with attachment of the elementary body to the host cell, followed by entry via receptor-mediated endocytosis. Inside the cell, it transforms into a reticulate body that divides every ~8 hours. After 18-24 hours, these bodies reorganize back into elementary bodies, which are then released to infect new cells.
keyPoints: Elementary body diameter: 0.25–0.35 µm
Reticulate body diameter: 0.5–1 µm
Reorganization time from reticulate to elementary body: ~18-24 hours
Binary fission of reticulate bodies begins after ~8 hours
Chlamydiae are energy parasites, relying on host ATP
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.title: Intracellular Survival Mechanisms of Chlamydiae
items.content: Chlamydiae prevent phagosome–lysosome fusion to survive within host cells. The inclusion membrane is modified by chlamydial antigens, allowing the organism to complete its growth cycle intracellularly. Despite possessing enzymes and being susceptible to broad-spectrum antibiotics, chlamydiae have limited metabolic capacity and rely on host ATP for energy.
keyPoints: Prevention of phagosome–lysosome fusion
Inclusion membrane modified by chlamydial antigens
Susceptibility to broad-spectrum antibiotics
Limited metabolic capacity, reliance on host ATP
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.title: Antigenic Variation in C. trachomatis
items.content: Polymorphisms in the major outer-membrane protein gene (omp1) of C. trachomatis lead to antigenic variation, allowing persistence within communities as immunity to one serovar does not confer protection against others.
keyPoints: C. trachomatis serovars D-K cause genital infections; serovars D, E, and F are most frequent.
Polymorphisms in omp1 gene lead to antigenic variation.
Immunity to one serovar does not protect against others.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.title: Immunopathologic Mechanisms in Chlamydial Infections
items.content: Chlamydial infections often result from immunopathologic mechanisms rather than direct cell death, with chronic and asymptomatic infections being common due to the organism's ability to persist.
keyPoints: C. trachomatis causes chronic, clinically inapparent, asymptomatic infections.
Disease manifestations are primarily due to immunopathologic mechanisms or host responses.
Chlamydial growth cycle is ~48–72 hours; incubation period for STIs is 1–3 weeks.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.title: Importance of Screening for Asymptomatic Chlamydial Infections
items.content: Chlamydia infections are often asymptomatic, with up to 80–90% of women and >50% of men lacking symptoms. This high rate of asymptomatic cases contributes to the transmission of chlamydia among sexual partners.
keyPoints: Up to 80-90% of women and >50% of men with C. trachomatis genital infections lack symptoms.
Asymptomatic nature leads to continued transmission without detection.
Annual screening recommended for sexually active women <25 years and those at increased risk, along with retesting previously infected individuals.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.title: Epidemiology of Chlamydial Infections
items.content: Chlamydia is the most prevalent bacterial STI globally, with significant regional variations in incidence rates.
keyPoints: In 2020, WHO estimated 50 million prevalent cases in men and 77 million in women globally.
59 million new male cases and 70 million new female cases reported in 2020.
Incidence per 1000 in the Americas: 48 for males, 68 for females; Southeast Asia: 12 for males, 16 for females.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.title: U.S. Chlamydia Surveillance and Screening Recommendations
items.content: Chlamydial infections are the most commonly reported infectious diseases in the U.S., with specific screening recommendations to mitigate transmission.
keyPoints: In 2022, 1,649,716 cases were reported, a 5-year decline of 6.2%.
CDC recommends annual screening for sexually active women <25 years and those at increased risk >25 years.
Pregnant women under 25 or at increased risk should be screened during the first prenatal visit.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.title: Screening Recommendations for Chlamydia
items.content: The CDC recommends annual screening for chlamydia in sexually active MSM, while evidence does not support general screening of young sexually active men.
keyPoints: CDC estimated 4 million cases of chlamydia in the U.S. in 2018.
Annual screening recommended for sexually active MSM at anatomically exposed sites.
Evidence lacking to support general screening of young sexually active men.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Risk Factors for C. trachomatis Infections
items.content: C. trachomatis infections are prevalent among young women aged 20-24, with higher rates in STI clinics and associated risk factors including bacterial vaginosis and high-risk HPV.
keyPoints: U.S. prevalence of C. trachomatis cervical infection was 5.9% in asymptomatic male college students and 8.8% in female college students.
Prevalence among prenatal patients ranges from 3.3 to 8.3%, and is 7% for women seen in family planning clinics, >10% for those in STI clinics.
C. trachomatis prevalence in pregnant women's cervix is six times higher than Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
Highest prevalence among women aged 20-24.
Associated with bacterial vaginosis and high-risk HPV.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of C. trachomatis Urethritis
items.content: C. trachomatis is the leading cause of nongonococcal urethritis, with a prevalence of 30-50% among NGU cases in men, and symptoms include urethral discharge and dysuria.
keyPoints: C. trachomatis is the most common cause of nongonococcal urethritis (NGU).
Prevalence of C. trachomatis in NGU ranges from 3-7% among asymptomatic men to 15-20% among symptomatic men at STI clinics.
Symptoms include urethral discharge, dysuria, and urethral itching.
NGU diagnosed by leukocytes on urethral exudate and exclusion of gonorrhea.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.title: Asymptomatic Chlamydial Infection in Males
items.content: A significant proportion of male patients with C. trachomatis urethral infection may not exhibit symptoms, making asymptomatic testing crucial.
keyPoints: At least one-third of males with C. trachomatis have no evident signs or symptoms of urethritis.
Asymptomatic chlamydial infection is found in 5–10% of sexually active male adolescents screened at school-based clinics or community centers.
Pyuria (≥15 leukocytes per 400 × microscopic field) and positive leukocyte esterase tests are indicators for asymptomatic infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Epididymitis
items.content: Chlamydial urethritis can lead to acute epididymitis, which requires prompt diagnosis and differentiation from other conditions.
keyPoints: Epididymitis is rare but occurs in 50–70% of cases due to C. trachomatis.
Typically presents with unilateral scrotal pain, tenderness, swelling, and fever.
Testicular torsion should be excluded promptly by imaging or surgical exploration.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.title: Reactive Arthritis Triggered by Chlamydial Infection
items.content: Chlamydia can trigger reactive arthritis, characterized by a triad of symptoms and associated with specific immune responses.
keyPoints: Reactive arthritis develops in 1–2% of NGU cases; chlamydia-induced arthritis rate is 4–15%.
C. trachomatis recovered from the urethra in 16–44% of reactive arthritis patients.
Chlamydial nucleic acids and elementary bodies found in synovial membranes and joint fluid.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.title: Reactive Arthritis Post-Urethritis
items.content: Arthritis can develop ~4 weeks after the onset of urethritis, often affecting the knees first, followed by ankles and small joints of the feet.
keyPoints: Arthritis usually begins ~4 weeks after urethritis.
Knees are most frequently involved; next are ankles and small joints of feet.
Sacroiliitis occurs in two-thirds of patients.
Dermatologic manifestations occur in up to 50% of patients.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.title: Diagnosis and Characteristics of LGV Proctitis
items.content: LGV proctitis is more invasive, often presenting with severe symptoms like ulcerative proctocolitis; it can be confused clinically with HSV proctitis.
keyPoints: LGV serovars are more invasive than non-LGV serovars.
Symptoms include anorectal pain, bloody mucopurulent discharge, and tenesmus.
Histologically resembles Crohn's disease with giant cell formation and granulomas.
NAATs of rectal swabs are the diagnostic test of choice.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.title: Diagnosis of Mucopurulent Cervicitis
items.content: Cervical mucus examination reveals >20 neutrophils per microscopic field, and a cutoff of >30 PMNs/1000× field correlates with chlamydial or gonococcal cervicitis.
keyPoints: Mucopurulent discharge characterized by >20 neutrophils in cervical mucus.
Cutoff of >30 PMNs/1000× field correlates best with infection.
NAATs are preferred for confirming chlamydiae presence.
Differential diagnosis includes gonococcal endocervicitis, salpingitis, and others.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.title: Chlamydial Infections and PID: Prevalence, Pathogenesis, and Prevention
items.content: C. trachomatis is a significant cause of pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), accounting for up to 50% of cases in the U.S. The pathogen ascends from the lower genital tract causing mucopurulent cervicitis, which can lead to endometritis and salpingitis. Screening and treating sexually active women for chlamydia and gonorrhea reduces PID risk.
keyPoints: C. trachomatis causes up to 50% of PID cases in the United States.
PID occurs via ascending intraluminal spread from lower genital tract infections.
Screening and treating sexually active women for chlamydia and gonorrhea reduces their risk for PID.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.title: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis Syndrome: Association with Chlamydial Infection
items.content: Perihepatitis, or Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome, is more commonly associated with chlamydial infection than gonococcal infection. It presents as right-upper-quadrant pain in young, sexually active women and can be linked to extensive tubal scarring observed at laparoscopy.
keyPoints: Chlamydial infection is more commonly associated with perihepatitis than N. gonorrhoeae.
Perihepatitis should be suspected in young, sexually active women with right-upper-quadrant pain, fever, or nausea.
Culture and/or serologic evidence of C. trachomatis has been found in three-fourths of women with Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.title: Chlamydial Urethral Syndrome: Diagnosis and Correlates
items.content: C. trachomatis is the most common pathogen in urethral syndrome among college women without uropathogens, presenting with dysuria and frequency. Mucopurulent cervicitis suggests chlamydial urethritis. Gram's stains showing >10 PMNs/1000× field support diagnosis.
keyPoints: C. trachomatis is the most common pathogen in urethral syndrome among college women without uropathogens.
Mucopurulent cervicitis in a woman with dysuria and frequency strongly suggests C. trachomatis urethritis.
Gram's stains showing >10 PMNs/1000× field support the diagnosis of chlamydial urethritis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.title: Prevention and Diagnosis of Neonatal Chlamydial Conjunctivitis
items.content: Chlamydia trachomatis can cause conjunctivitis in newborns exposed during delivery, with a significant percentage developing the condition. Prophylaxis at birth is ineffective against chlamydial infections, emphasizing the importance of screening and treating pregnant women.
keyPoints: 20–30% of infants develop conjunctivitis when exposed to C. trachomatis in the birth canal.
10–15% of these infants subsequently develop pneumonia.
Ocular prophylaxis at birth prevents gonococcal but not chlamydial conjunctivitis.
Laboratory diagnosis is required as clinical differentiation from other neonatal conjunctivitis forms is impossible.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.title: Chlamydial Infections and Long-term Pulmonary Consequences in Infants
items.content: C. trachomatis can persistently infect infants' nasopharynx, rectum, and vagina, leading to pneumonia and potential long-term pulmonary issues such as bronchitis or asthma.
keyPoints: Chlamydial pneumonia occurs in 20–30% of cases in infants <6 months old.
Pneumonia typically develops from 2 weeks to 4 months of age.
Epidemiologic studies link chlamydial pulmonary infection with increased subacute lung disease later in childhood.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.title: Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV) Presentation and Diagnosis
items.content: LGV, caused by specific C. trachomatis serovars, often presents with asymptomatic rectal infections in MSM, necessitating NAATs for accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: Serovars L1, L2, and L3 cause LGV, an invasive STI.
LGV is increasingly prevalent among MSM, often presenting as asymptomatic rectal infection (30–50% of cases).
NAATs are crucial for identifying rectal infections, enhancing case recognition.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.title: Inguinal Syndrome in LGV: Presentation and Complications
items.content: Lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) often presents as inguinal syndrome, characterized by painful lymphadenopathy occurring 2–6 weeks post-exposure. It is unilateral in two-thirds of cases with potential progression to matted, fluctuant, and suppurative nodes.
keyPoints: Inguinal lymphadenopathy begins 2-6 weeks after exposure; can be delayed by months.
Unilateral involvement occurs in two-thirds of cases.
Progression leads to a matted mass with draining fistulas.
Spontaneous healing is common, but inguinal scars or granulomatous masses persist.
Massive pelvic lymphadenopathy may necessitate exploratory laparotomy.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.title: Systemic and Anorectal Complications of LGV
items.content: LGV can cause systemic symptoms like fever, chills, and arthralgias during lymphadenopathy. Untreated anorectal infections may lead to severe complications such as perirectal abscesses and fistulas.
keyPoints: Constitutional symptoms include fever, headache, myalgias, and arthralgias.
Complications of untreated anorectal infection: perirectal abscess, anal fistulas.
Rectal stricture typically develops 2-6 cm from the anal orifice.
Chronic LGV in men may cause urethral strictures leading to urinary issues.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.title: Diagnosis of Chlamydial Infections: NAATs and Specimen Choice
items.content: NAATs are the preferred diagnostic method for chlamydia, replacing older culture techniques. The choice of specimen varies by patient gender and clinical setting.
keyPoints: NAATs recommended over culture methods; FDA-cleared assays available.
Cervical swabs slightly more sensitive than urine for symptomatic women.
Vaginal swabs preferred for screening in women, per CDC recommendations.
First-catch urine is the sample of choice for male patients.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.title: Use of NAATs in Test-of-Cure for Chlamydial Infections
items.content: NAATs should not be used as a test-of-cure for chlamydia until at least 3 weeks post-treatment due to the potential persistence of nucleic acids from noninfective cells, which can yield false-positive results.
keyPoints: NAATs detect nucleic acids and may remain positive up to 3 weeks after therapy.
CDC does not recommend a test-of-cure for C. trachomatis except in pregnancy.
Previously infected individuals should be rescreened 3 months post-treatment due to high reinfection risk.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.title: Role of Serologic Testing in Chlamydial Infections
items.content: Serologic testing, particularly the MIF test, is useful for diagnosing LGV and neonatal pneumonia caused by C. trachomatis but not recommended for uncomplicated genital infections or asymptomatic screening.
keyPoints: MIF test is preferred for serology in LGV diagnosis; CF test identifies only to genus level.
Single-point titers >1:64 support LGV diagnosis, while titer >1:16 indicates exposure to chlamydiae.
Serologic testing is not recommended for diagnosing uncomplicated genital infections or asymptomatic screening.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.title: Optimal Treatment Regimens for Uncomplicated Chlamydial Infections
items.content: The recommended treatment for uncomplicated chlamydial infections is a 7-day course of oral doxycycline (100 mg twice daily). Alternatives include a single 1-g dose of azithromycin or a 7-day course of oral levofloxacin (500 mg once daily). Doxycycline shows slightly better efficacy than azithromycin, especially for rectal infections. Azithromycin's single-dose regimen is advantageous for patients with adherence challenges and sexual partners of infected individuals, though it has lower efficacy and higher cost.
keyPoints: 7-day course of oral doxycycline (100 mg twice daily) recommended
Alternatives: 1-g azithromycin single dose or 7 days of levofloxacin (500 mg once daily)
Doxycycline has better efficacy than azithromycin for genital and rectal infections
Azithromycin's single-dose regimen is beneficial for adherence challenges
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.title: Treatment Considerations in Special Populations
items.content: For pregnant women, amoxicillin (500 mg three times daily for 7 days) is recommended as an alternative to doxycycline or azithromycin, which are not FDA-approved for pregnancy. Fluoroquinolones are contraindicated during pregnancy. A 2-week treatment course is advised for complicated infections like PID and epididymitis. For LGV, a traditional 3-week regimen includes doxycycline (100 mg twice daily), azithromycin (1 g once weekly), or erythromycin base (500 mg four times daily). Shorter-course treatments have shown efficacy in some studies.
keyPoints: Amoxicillin (500 mg three times daily for 7 days) recommended for pregnant women
Fluoroquinolones contraindicated in pregnancy
2-week treatment course for complicated infections like PID and epididymitis
Traditional 3-week LGV regimen: doxycycline, azithromycin, or erythromycin base
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.title: Partner-Delivered Therapy for Chlamydial Infections
items.content: Index patients with chlamydial infections should receive treatment and be provided with medication to administer to their sex partners, even if the partners do not have confirmed infection. This approach helps address asymptomatic cases that contribute to ongoing transmission.
keyPoints: Partner-delivered therapy involves treating index patients and providing them with medication for their partners.
This strategy is used when partners are exposed but may not show symptoms or test positive.
It addresses the high prevalence of chlamydial infections by targeting asymptomatic carriers.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.title: Erythromycin Treatment for Neonatal Chlamydial Infections
items.content: Neonates with conjunctivitis or infants with pneumonia should receive oral erythromycin ethylsuccinate at 50 mg/kg per day in four divided doses for two weeks. Follow-up is crucial due to potential relapses, and the efficacy of this treatment is approximately 80%.
keyPoints: Erythromycin dosage: 50 mg/kg/day, divided into four doses for 2 weeks.
Follow-up required due to ~80% efficacy and risk of relapse.
Both parents should be examined or treated if testing isn't available.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.title: Chlamydia Screening Recommendations
items.content: Selective screening for chlamydial infections is effective in reducing prevalence, especially among women under 25. Universal screening becomes more cost-effective than selective screening when prevalence exceeds 3-7%. The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends Chlamydia screening as a Grade B recommendation.
keyPoints: Selective screening reduces chlamydial infection prevalence.
Universal screening is preferable at >3–7% prevalence.
Grade B recommendation by the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force ensures coverage under the Affordable Care Act.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.title: Epidemiology and Global Impact of Trachoma
items.content: Trachoma remains a leading cause of preventable infectious blindness, particularly in resource-limited areas. The WHO estimates significant global impact with millions affected.
keyPoints: ~6 million people have been blinded by trachoma globally.
~1.3 million people suffer from preventable blindness due to trachoma in developing countries.
Trachoma is endemic in Africa, Asia, Latin America, the Middle East, and the Pacific Rim.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.title: Transmission and Prevention of Trachoma
items.content: Trachoma spreads through contact with eye discharges under poor hygienic conditions. Efforts like the WHO's International Trachoma Initiative aim to eliminate it as a public health problem by 2030.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs via eye discharges and flies (e.g., Musca sorbens).
WHO aimed for global elimination of blinding trachoma by 2020, now extended to 2030.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Trachoma
items.content: Trachoma progresses from active conjunctivitis to cicatricial disease causing blindness. Diagnosis involves clinical signs and laboratory confirmation.
keyPoints: Active trachoma presents as conjunctivitis with lymphoid follicles.
Cicatricial disease leads to scarring, trichiasis, entropion, and potential blindness.
Diagnosis confirmed by two of four clinical signs: lymphoid follicles, conjunctival scarring, vascular pannus, or limbal follicles/Herbert pits.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.title: Effective Treatment Regimens for Adult Inclusion Conjunctivitis Due to Chlamydia
items.content: Adult inclusion conjunctivitis, caused by chlamydial infection, is effectively treated with systemic antibiotics such as azithromycin or doxycycline. Topical treatments are unnecessary if systemic therapy is administered.
keyPoints: Azithromycin: 1-g single oral dose
Doxycycline: 100 mg twice daily for 7 days
Topical tetracycline (1% eye ointment) as an alternative, but adherence may be low
Simultaneous treatment of all sexual partners is necessary to prevent reinfection
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Presentation of Psittacosis in Humans
items.content: Psittacosis, a zoonotic infection from C. psittaci, is diagnosed serologically and can present as acute atypical pneumonia with up to 10% fatality if untreated, chronic severe pneumonia, or mild/asymptomatic illness.
keyPoints: Diagnosis established serologically
Acute primary atypical pneumonia: fatal in up to 10% of untreated cases
Transmission via direct contact or inhalation from birds
No person-to-person transmission documented
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.title: Control Measures for Psittacosis: Importance of Avian Source Management
items.content: Psittacosis control relies heavily on managing avian sources due to its transmission from birds. Historically, a pandemic was halted by banning the shipment or importation of psittacine birds. Current measures include quarantining imported birds for 30 days with tetracycline-containing feed.
keyPoints: CDC reports approximately 10 confirmed cases annually since 2010.
Outbreaks have been observed in poultry plant workers.
Imported birds are quarantined for 30 days of treatment.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis of Psittacosis: Serologic Testing and Interpretation
items.content: Serologic testing is crucial for diagnosing psittacosis, with the MIF test being the gold standard despite limited availability. A significant antibody titer above 1:16 indicates exposure to chlamydiae, and a fourfold rise in paired sera confirms diagnosis when combined with compatible clinical symptoms.
keyPoints: CF test shows fourfold or greater increases in antibody titer for diagnosis.
MIF test is the gold standard but not widely available.
Antibody titer above 1:16 indicates exposure; a fourfold rise confirms diagnosis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Doxycycline as First-Line Treatment for Psittacosis
items.content: For treating psittacosis, doxycycline is the preferred antibiotic due to its efficacy in managing the infection. The recommended dosage for adults is 100 mg taken twice daily over a course of 10–21 days.
keyPoints: Doxycycline is the antibiotic of choice for psittacosis.
Adult dosage: 100 mg twice a day for 10–21 days.
Intravenous doxycycline may be necessary for severely ill patients.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Prevalence and Seroprevalence of C. pneumoniae Infections
items.content: C. pneumoniae is a significant cause of respiratory diseases, contributing to 1–2% of community-acquired pneumonia cases. It has widespread seroprevalence, with rates between 40–70%, indicating its commonality across various age groups and regions.
keyPoints: C. pneumoniae accounts for 1–2% of community-acquired pneumonia cases.
Seroprevalence rates range from 40–70% globally.
Seropositivity increases by ~10% per decade, with most individuals having detectable antibodies by age 80.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: C. pneumoniae and Autoimmune Reactions
items.content: C. pneumoniae infection may induce autoimmune reactions due to cross-reactivity between a C. pneumoniae outer-membrane protein and human proteins.
keyPoints: C. pneumoniae begins in the upper respiratory tract.
The organism can persist asymptomatically on mucosal surfaces.
Cross-reactivity of host immune responses with human proteins may lead to autoimmune reactions.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: C. pneumoniae and Atherosclerosis
items.content: There is an association between C. pneumoniae infection and atherosclerotic disease, but the etiologic role remains uncertain.
keyPoints: Association with coronary heart disease and acute myocardial infarction since 1988.
Identified in atherosclerotic lesions by culture, PCR, immunohistochemistry, and electron microscopy.
Viable replicating bacteria found in atheromatous plaques.
Animal models show infection accelerates atherosclerosis, especially in hypercholesterolemic animals.
Antimicrobial treatment in animal models reverses increased risk of atherosclerosis.
Small human trials suggest antibiotics may reduce cardiac events post-infection; larger trials are inconclusive.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Severity of C. pneumoniae Infection in Different Populations
items.content: C. pneumoniae primarily affects young adults with symptoms like acute pharyngitis, sinusitis, bronchitis, and pneumonitis. Primary infections are more severe and prolonged than reinfections. Elderly patients may experience particularly severe pneumonia requiring hospitalization.
keyPoints: Primary infection is more severe and prolonged compared to reinfection.
Elderly patients may require hospitalization due to severe pneumonia.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Preferred Diagnostic Methods for C. pneumoniae Infection
items.content: PCR amplification of respiratory secretions is the preferred diagnostic method for C. pneumoniae infection, with several commercial assays available. Serology and culture are alternatives but have limitations; serology lacks FDA approval due to poor predictive value.
keyPoints: PCR is the preferred diagnostic method.
Serology is not routinely used clinically due to poor predictive value.
Any antibody titer >1:16 indicates exposure to chlamydiae.
Diagnosis of acute infection requires a fourfold rise in titer in paired serum samples.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which species within the genus Chlamydia is primarily associated with causing trachoma and urogenital infections in humans?
items.explanation: C. trachomatis is divided into two biovars: trachoma and LGV, which cause ocular trachoma and urogenital infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.question: How many serovars of C. trachomatis are classified under the trachoma group?
items.explanation: The trachoma serovars include A, B, Ba, and C.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary diagnostic method for visualizing chlamydiae in infected clinical material?
options: Direct fluorescent antibody or Giemsa staining
items.explanation: Chlamydiae produce characteristic intracytoplasmic inclusions that can be visualized by direct fluorescent antibody or Giemsa staining.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.question: Which form of Chlamydia is specifically adapted for extracellular survival?
items.explanation: The elementary body is the infectious form of Chlamydia, adapted for extracellular survival.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.question: What process does the elementary body use to enter the host cell?
items.explanation: The elementary body enters the host cell through a process similar to receptor-mediated endocytosis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.question: What prevents chlamydiae from being destroyed by the host's immune system?
items.explanation: Chlamydiae prevent phagosome–lysosome fusion, allowing them to survive within the host cell.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.question: Which serovars of C. trachomatis are most frequently involved in genital infections?
items.explanation: Genital infections are primarily caused by C. trachomatis serovars D-K.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for sexually transmitted chlamydial infections?
items.explanation: The incubation period of sexually transmitted chlamydial infections is generally 1–3 weeks.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.question: What contributes to the persistence of C. trachomatis in the community?
options: Antigenic variation due to polymorphisms in the major outer-membrane protein
High mutation rate in DNA replication
Absence of cell death during infection
items.explanation: Polymorphisms in the major outer-membrane protein may provide antigenic variation, allowing persistence because immunity to one form is not protective against others.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical consequence of untreated C. trachomatis genital infections in women?
items.explanation: Untreated C. trachomatis infections can lead to PID, tubal factor infertility, and chronic pelvic pain, but not acute myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of women with C. trachomatis genital infections typically lack symptoms?
items.explanation: Studies estimate that up to 80–90% of women with C. trachomatis genital infections lack symptoms.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.question: Which serovars of C. trachomatis cause Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV)?
items.explanation: The LGV serovars ($ L_{1} $, $ L_{2} $, and $ L_{3} $) cause Lymphogranuloma Venereum.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended frequency of chlamydia screening for sexually active women under 25 years in the U.S.?
items.explanation: The CDC recommends annual screening of all sexually active women under 25 years.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.question: What was the estimated annual cost to the U.S. health care system due to C. trachomatis infections and their complications in recent years?
items.explanation: The passage states that the costs of C. trachomatis infections and their complications to the U.S. health care system have been estimated to be >$516.7 million annually.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.question: Which sexually transmitted infection is most commonly associated with nongonococcal urethritis (NGU)?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that C. trachomatis is the most common cause of nongonococcal urethritis (NGU).
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.question: What percentage range of NGU cases in symptomatic men attending STI clinics is caused by C. trachomatis?
items.explanation: The passage states that the rate of involvement of C. trachomatis in urethral infection ranges from 15–20% among symptomatic men attending STI clinics.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.question: Which age group has the highest prevalence of genital infection with C. trachomatis?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that the prevalence of genital infection with either agent is highest among women who are between the ages of 20 and 24.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of male patients with C. trachomatis urethral infection typically have no evident signs or symptoms?
items.explanation: At least one-third of male patients with C. trachomatis urethral infection have no evident signs or symptoms.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.question: What is the estimated percentage range of acute epididymitis cases caused by C. trachomatis?
items.explanation: An estimated 50–70% of cases of acute epididymitis are caused by C. trachomatis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.question: What is the rate at which reactive arthritis develops in cases of NGU?
items.explanation: Reactive arthritis consists of conjunctivitis, urethritis (or cervicitis), arthritis, and characteristic mucocutaneous lesions; it may develop in 1–2% of cases of NGU.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.question: What is the typical time frame within which NGU occurs as an initial manifestation of reactive arthritis after sexual exposure?
items.explanation: NGU is the initial manifestation of reactive arthritis in 80% of patients, typically occurring within 14 days after sexual exposure.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.question: Which serovars of C. trachomatis are primarily responsible for causing proctitis in MSM?
options: Oculogenital serovars D-K and LGV serovars L1, L2, and L3
Only LGV serovars L1, L2, and L3
Chlamydial infections do not cause proctitis
items.explanation: Both oculogenital serovars D-K and LGV serovars L1, L2, and L3 can cause proctitis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.question: What is the typical duration of an initial episode of reactive arthritis associated with chlamydial infections?
items.explanation: The initial episode of reactive arthritis usually lasts 2–6 months.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.question: Which diagnostic test is considered the best for diagnosing chlamydial proctitis?
options: Isolation of C. trachomatis from the rectum and response to therapy
items.explanation: Chlamydial proctitis is best diagnosed by isolation of C. trachomatis from the rectum and documentation of a response to appropriate therapy.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.question: What histologic finding is characteristic of LGV proctitis?
options: Neutrophilic infiltration of the lamina propria
Prominent follicles in the lower rectum
items.explanation: LGV proctitis may resemble Crohn's disease with giant cell formation and granulomas.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with perihepatitis in young, sexually active women?
items.explanation: Chlamydial infection is more commonly associated with perihepatitis than N. gonorrhoeae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.question: What percentage of PID cases in the United States are estimated to be caused by C. trachomatis?
items.explanation: C. trachomatis causes up to 50% of PID cases in the United States.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.question: What is a common finding in women with laparoscopically verified salpingitis?
options: Endometrial biopsy showing plasma cell infiltration
Histologic evidence of endometritis without adnexal tenderness
items.explanation: Evidence of mucopurulent cervicitis and histologic evidence of endometritis are often found in women with laparoscopically verified salpingitis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.question: Which symptom is less likely to be associated with chlamydial salpingitis compared to gonococcal salpingitis?
items.explanation: Chlamydial salpingitis produces milder symptoms than gonococcal salpingitis and may be associated with less marked adnexal tenderness.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.question: What is the estimated percentage of infants exposed to C. trachomatis in the birth canal who develop pneumonia?
items.explanation: Approximately 10–15% of infants exposed to C. trachomatis in the birth canal subsequently develop pneumonia.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.question: Which diagnostic method is more sensitive than Giemsa-stained conjunctival smears for detecting chlamydial conjunctivitis in newborns?
items.explanation: Giemsa-stained conjunctival smears are considerably less sensitive than cultures or NAATs for chlamydiae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.question: What is the most effective measure to prevent neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis?
options: Use of agents against gonococcal infection
Screening and treatment of pregnant women
items.explanation: The most effective measure to prevent neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis is through screening and treatment of chlamydial infections in pregnant women.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.question: Which C. trachomatis serovars cause lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV)?
items.explanation: C. trachomatis serovars $ L_{1} $, $ L_{2} $, and $ L_{3} $ cause LGV.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.question: What is the most common presenting picture in heterosexual men and women with chlamydial infections?
options: Painful inguinal lymphadenopathy beginning 2–6 weeks after presumed exposure
Immediate onset of systemic symptoms such as fever and headache
Chronic progressive infiltrative lesions of the penis or scrotum
Massive pelvic lymphadenopathy leading to exploratory laparotomy
items.explanation: The most common presenting picture is painful inguinal lymphadenopathy beginning 2–6 weeks after presumed exposure.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.question: Which of the following is a late complication of untreated anorectal chlamydial infection?
items.explanation: Rectal stricture is a late complication of untreated anorectal infection.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.question: Which diagnostic method has replaced cell culture as the gold standard for detecting chlamydiae?
items.explanation: NAATs have replaced cell culture as the diagnostic gold standard for detecting chlamydiae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.question: What is the CDC's current recommendation for screening women for chlamydial infections?
options: Vaginal swabs, either self-collected or clinician-collected
items.explanation: The CDC recommends using vaginal swabs, which are slightly more sensitive than urine.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.question: Why should NAATs be used with caution as test-of-cure assays for chlamydial infections?
options: NAATs can detect nucleic acids from noninfective cells up to 3 weeks after therapy.
NAATs are not sensitive enough to detect low levels of infection.
NAATs require a longer time to process results compared to other tests.
NAATs cannot differentiate between different species of Chlamydia.
items.explanation: Residual nucleic acid from noninfective cells can yield positive NAAT results up to 3 weeks after therapy.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.question: What does the CDC recommend regarding test of cure for C. trachomatis infection?
options: A test of cure is recommended immediately after treatment.
A test of cure is not recommended except in pregnancy.
A test of cure should be conducted 1 week post-treatment.
A test of cure is mandatory for all treated individuals.
items.explanation: The CDC does not recommend a test of cure after treatment, except in pregnancy.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.question: For which conditions is serologic testing with MIF recommended?
options: Uncomplicated genital infections of the cervix and urethra.
LGV and neonatal pneumonia caused by C. trachomatis.
Diagnosis of all types of chlamydial infections.
items.explanation: Serologic testing with MIF is recommended for LGV and neonatal pneumonia caused by C. trachomatis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for uncomplicated chlamydial infections?
options: A 7-day course of oral doxycycline (100 mg twice daily)
A single 1-g oral dose of azithromycin
Oral levofloxacin 500 mg once daily for 7 days
Amoxicillin (500 mg three times daily for 7 days)
items.explanation: The recommended first-line treatment is a 7-day course of oral doxycycline.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen has been shown to have higher microbiological cure rates for rectal chlamydial infections?
items.explanation: Doxycycline has demonstrated significantly higher microbiological cure rates for rectal chlamydial infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.question: Which treatment is contraindicated in pregnancy due to potential risks?
items.explanation: Fluoroquinolones, including levofloxacin, are contraindicated in pregnancy.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended for oral treatment in neonates with conjunctivitis or infants with pneumonia due to chlamydial infection?
items.explanation: Erythromycin ethylsuccinate is given orally for neonates with conjunctivitis or infants with pneumonia.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.question: What is the approximate efficacy of erythromycin treatment for ophthalmia neonatorum?
items.explanation: The efficacy of erythromycin treatment for ophthalmia neonatorum is ~80%.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.question: What strategy involves index patients receiving treatment and providing it to their sex partners?
items.explanation: Partner-delivered therapy involves index patients receiving treatment and giving it to their sex partner(s).
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.question: In which settings is universal screening for chlamydial infection considered more cost-effective?
options: Settings with no known risk factors
items.explanation: Universal screening is preferable in settings with a chlamydial prevalence of >3–7%.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.question: Which C. trachomatis serovars are isolated from patients with clinical trachoma in endemic areas?
options: Serovars A, B, Ba, and C
items.explanation: C. trachomatis serovars A, B, Ba, and C are isolated from patients with clinical trachoma in endemic areas.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for trachoma?
items.explanation: Trachoma is transmitted through contact with discharges from the eyes of infected patients.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.question: Which age group is most commonly affected by active trachoma?
items.explanation: Active disease is most common among young children, who are the reservoir for trachoma.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.question: What clinical manifestation indicates active trachoma?
items.explanation: Active trachoma is characterized by conjunctivitis with small lymphoid follicles in the conjunctiva.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen is recommended for treating adult inclusion conjunctivitis due to Chlamydia?
options: Azithromycin (a 1-g single oral dose) or doxycycline (100 mg twice daily for 7 days)
Topical tetracycline (1% eye ointment twice daily for 6 weeks)
Ciprofloxacin (500 mg twice daily for 10 days)
Amoxicillin (500 mg three times a day for 14 days)
items.explanation: Azithromycin or doxycycline are recommended for adult inclusion conjunctivitis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission of C. psittaci to humans?
options: Direct contact with infected birds or inhalation of aerosols from avian discharges
items.explanation: C. psittaci is transmitted through direct contact with birds or inhalation of aerosols.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.question: What type of pneumonia can C. psittaci cause in humans?
items.explanation: C. psittaci can cause acute primary atypical pneumonia.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.question: Which of the following measures has historically helped control outbreaks of psittacosis?
options: Routine testing for all poultry workers
Banning shipment or importation of psittacine birds
Administering antibiotics to humans in endemic areas
items.explanation: A pandemic of psittacosis was once stopped by banning shipment or importation of psittacine birds.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the gold standard serologic test for diagnosing chlamydial infections?
items.explanation: The MIF test is now considered the gold standard of serologic tests for diagnosing chlamydial infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a significant antibody titer level that indicates exposure to chlamydiae?
items.explanation: Any antibody titer above 1:16 is considered significant evidence of exposure to chlamydiae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the antibiotic of choice for treating psittacosis?
items.explanation: Doxycycline is identified as the antibiotic of choice for psittacosis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which diagnostic method is preferred for acute infections caused by C. pneumoniae?
items.explanation: PCR is the preferred method for diagnosing acute C. pneumoniae infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of community-acquired pneumonia cases are typically caused by C. pneumoniae?
items.explanation: C. pneumoniae accounts for 1–2% of community-acquired pneumonia cases, typically.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a proposed mechanism by which C. pneumoniae may contribute to autoimmune reactions?
options: A C. pneumoniae outer-membrane protein induces host immune responses that cross-react with human proteins.
C. pneumoniae directly infects and destroys human cells.
C. pneumoniae produces toxins that damage the vascular endothelium.
C. pneumoniae prevents macrophages from engulfing bacteria.
items.explanation: A C. pneumoniae outer-membrane protein may induce host immune responses whose cross-reactivity with human proteins results in an autoimmune reaction.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What evidence supports the hypothesis that C. pneumoniae infection accelerates atherosclerosis?
options: C. pneumoniae was identified in atherosclerotic lesions by culture.
Animal models show recovery of C. pneumoniae from atheromatous lesions and accelerated atherosclerosis, especially in hypercholesterolemic animals.
Epidemiologic studies showed no association between C. pneumoniae infection and atherosclerosis.
Antimicrobial treatment has been proven to reduce the risk of cardiac events in large-scale trials.
items.explanation: Evidence from animal models supports that C. pneumoniae infection is followed by recovery of the organism from atheromatous lesions and accelerates atherosclerosis, especially in hypercholesterolemic animals.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What has been observed regarding the effect of antimicrobial treatment on C. pneumoniae-related atherosclerosis?
options: Antimicrobial treatment increases the risk of atherosclerosis.
Antimicrobial treatment reverses the increased risk of atherosclerosis in animal models.
Large-scale trials have confirmed that antibiotics reduce cardiac events in humans with C. pneumoniae infection.
Antimicrobial treatment has no effect on atherosclerosis.
items.explanation: In animal models, antimicrobial treatment of infected animals reverses the increased risk of atherosclerosis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which clinical manifestation is more severe in primary C. pneumoniae infection compared to reinfection?
items.explanation: Primary C. pneumoniae infection has more severe and prolonged clinical manifestations compared to reinfection.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is preferred for identifying C. pneumoniae in respiratory secretions?
items.explanation: PCR amplification of respiratory secretions is the preferred method for diagnosing C. pneumoniae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a significant indicator of exposure to chlamydiae according to serology?
items.explanation: Any antibody titer >1:16 is considered significant evidence of exposure to chlamydiae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: C. trachomatis serovars require different antibiotic treatments based on their group.
items.explanation: The antibiotic susceptibility pattern is the same for all three groups of C. trachomatis, except when LGV is suspected.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.statement: Chlamydiae possess both RNA and DNA.
items.explanation: Chlamydiae are nonmotile, gram-negative bacteria that have both RNA and DNA.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Chlamydial Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENTS | BIOLOGY

items.statement: The reticulate body is infectious and survives well outside the host cell.
items.explanation: The reticulate body is not infectious and does not survive well outside the host cell.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.statement: Chlamydiae use ATP produced by the host cell for their metabolic functions.
items.explanation: Chlamydiae are energy parasites that utilize the ATP from the host cell.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GROWTH CYCLE

items.statement: C. trachomatis infections are primarily symptomatic.
items.explanation: Chlamydiae can cause chronic, clinically inapparent, asymptomatic infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.statement: The trachoma biovar of C. trachomatis is more invasive than the LGV biovar.
items.explanation: The trachoma biovar is a parasite of squamocolumnar epithelial cells, while the LGV biovar is more invasive and involves lymphoid cells.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS | 2. Initial inclusions

items.statement: Chlamydial infections are the most commonly reported infectious disease in the United States.
items.explanation: In the U.S., chlamydia is the most commonly reported of all infectious diseases.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.statement: The incidence rate of chlamydial infection in men was higher than in women in the U.S. in 2022.
items.explanation: In 2022, women had a higher infection rate (621.2 cases per 100,000) compared to men (363.7 cases per 100,000).
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.statement: The prevalence of C. trachomatis in the cervix of pregnant women is lower than that of Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence of C. trachomatis in the cervix of pregnant women is approximately six times higher than that of Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.statement: C. trachomatis urethritis symptoms include frank purulent discharge.
items.explanation: The passage describes the discharge in C. trachomatis urethritis as often whitish and mucoid rather than frankly purulent.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.statement: Urine testing for urethral STIs in men is less acceptable than endourethral swab collection.
items.explanation: Urine testing for urethral STIs in men is much more acceptable than endourethral swab collection.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.statement: Reactive arthritis can be triggered by infections associated with bacterial gastroenteritis.
items.explanation: The pathogenesis of reactive arthritis probably represents an abnormal host response to a number of infectious agents, including those associated with bacterial gastroenteritis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.statement: Asymptomatic LGV infection is rarely recognized in MSM.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic or paucisymptomatic LGV infection is increasingly recognized in MSM.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.statement: Mucopurulent cervicitis can be diagnosed solely based on clinical symptoms.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of chlamydial cervicitis requires the presence of PMNs on a cervical swab and confirmation by NAAT.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.statement: Chlamydial endometritis can occur without clinical evidence of salpingitis.
items.explanation: Chlamydial endometritis can indeed occur in the absence of clinical evidence of salpingitis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.statement: Gonococcal infection is more commonly associated with perihepatitis than chlamydial infection.
items.explanation: Chlamydial infection, not gonococcal infection, is more commonly associated with perihepatitis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.statement: Ocular prophylaxis at birth is effective in preventing chlamydial conjunctivitis.
items.explanation: The agents used to prevent gonococcal eye infection are not effective against chlamydial conjunctivitis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.statement: LGV is more prevalent in industrialized countries.
items.explanation: LGV is rare in industrialized countries.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.statement: NAATs for chlamydia detection from conjunctival swabs are cleared by the FDA.
items.explanation: NAATs are not cleared by the FDA for detecting chlamydia from conjunctival swabs.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.statement: Systemic complications of chlamydial infections include arthritis with sterile effusion and aseptic meningitis.
items.explanation: Arthritis with sterile effusion and aseptic meningitis are listed as systemic complications.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.statement: The CDC recommends rescreening previously infected individuals for chlamydial infection 3 months after treatment.
items.explanation: Rescreening is recommended due to the increased probability of reinfection.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.statement: Serologic testing is recommended for diagnosing uncomplicated genital infections caused by C. trachomatis.
items.explanation: Serologic testing is not recommended for these types of infections.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 8)

items.statement: Clinically significant drug resistance has been observed in C. trachomatis.
items.explanation: To date, clinically significant drug resistance has not been observed in C. trachomatis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.statement: A single 1-g dose of azithromycin is recommended for the treatment of adult inclusion conjunctivitis.
items.explanation: A single 1-g dose of azithromycin was found to be as effective as a standard 10-day treatment with doxycycline.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · C. trachomatis Genital Infections

items.statement: Selective screening for chlamydia has been shown to reduce the incidence of symptomatic PID in women.
items.explanation: Screening can reduce upper genital tract disease, including symptomatic PID.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.statement: Doxycycline postexposure prophylaxis is effective for reducing chlamydia acquisition risk in all populations.
items.explanation: Efficacy data exist specifically for MSM and transgender women, not all populations.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.statement: Trachoma can be transmitted through eye-seeking flies.
items.explanation: Eye-seeking flies like Musca sorbens can transfer mucopurulent ocular discharges, carrying the organisms from one person to another.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.statement: Blinding trachoma is no longer a public health problem as of 2020.
items.explanation: The target year for global elimination of trachoma as a public health problem was set to 2030.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.statement: Chlamydial resistance to azithromycin has been documented.
items.explanation: Resistance to azithromycin in Chlamydia has not been documented.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.statement: Transmission of C. psittaci from person to person has been demonstrated.
items.explanation: Person-to-person transmission of C. psittaci has never been demonstrated.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Trachoma | PSITTACOSIS

items.statement: NAATs from respiratory samples are widely available in laboratories.
items.explanation: NAATs from respiratory samples are highly sensitive and specific but are not widely available in laboratories.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Fatal cases of psittacosis were common in the preantibiotic era.
items.explanation: Fatal cases were indeed common in the preantibiotic era.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Seroprevalence rates for C. pneumoniae are generally lower in developing countries compared to industrialized ones.
items.explanation: C. pneumoniae has seroprevalence rates of 40–70% in both industrialized and developing countries.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Most individuals have detectable antibody to C. pneumoniae by the eighth decade of life.
items.explanation: The passage states that most individuals have detectable antibody to C. pneumoniae by the eighth decade of life.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: C. pneumoniae has been consistently shown to reduce the likelihood of subsequent cardiac events in large-scale human trials.
items.explanation: Larger-scale trials have not documented an effect of various antichlamydial regimens on the risk of these events.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: C. pneumoniae has been identified in atherosclerotic plaques by multiple methods, including electron microscopy and DNA hybridization.
items.explanation: The organism has been identified in atherosclerotic plaques by electron microscopy, DNA hybridization, and immunocytochemistry.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Serology is the preferred method for diagnosing C. pneumoniae infection.
items.explanation: PCR amplification is the preferred diagnostic method, not serology.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: C. pneumoniae can cause severe pneumonia in elderly patients.
items.explanation: Pneumonia due to C. pneumoniae can be especially severe in elderly patients.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Chlamydial infections can persist for years without treatment.
items.reason: Extragenital infection in the pharynx and rectum is typically asymptomatic.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why chlamydial infections can persist without symptoms.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.assertion: The highest rates of chlamydia in the U.S. are among adolescents and young adults.
items.reason: This age group is more likely to engage in risky sexual behaviors.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason provided is not explicitly stated as a cause for the high rates.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 1)

items.assertion: Screening programs have demonstrated reductions in PID rates.
items.reason: There is evidence to support general screening of young sexually active men.
items.explanation: While the assertion that screening programs reduce PID rates is true, there is no supporting evidence for general screening of young sexually active men.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.assertion: The proportion of asymptomatic infections is higher for C. trachomatis than for N. gonorrhoeae.
items.reason: Asymptomatic C. trachomatis infections are clinically more severe.
items.explanation: Although the assertion about a higher proportion of asymptomatic infections for C. trachomatis is true, it is false that these infections are clinically more severe; they are less severe.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 2)

items.assertion: Chlamydial nucleic acids have been identified in synovial membranes.
items.reason: This finding suggests that chlamydiae may spread from genital to joint tissues, perhaps in macrophages.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.assertion: An association between reactive arthritis and the HLA-B27 phenotype has been described in sub-Saharan Africa.
items.reason: The prevalence of HLA-B27 is much lower in patients from sub-Saharan Africa.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; there is no association described between reactive arthritis and HLA-B27 phenotype in sub-Saharan Africa.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 3)

items.assertion: Chlamydial infections can cause reactive arthritis.
items.reason: Arthritis usually begins ~4 weeks after the onset of urethritis but may develop sooner or, in a small percentage of cases, precede it.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.assertion: LGV serovars cause less symptomatic proctitis compared to non-LGV serovars.
items.reason: LGV serovars are far more invasive and typically cause more severely symptomatic disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 4)

items.assertion: Screening and treating sexually active women for chlamydia and gonorrhea can reduce their risk for PID.
items.reason: Chronic untreated endometrial and tubal inflammation can lead to infertility.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.assertion: C. trachomatis is the pathogen most commonly isolated from college women with dysuria, frequency, and pyuria.
items.reason: Coliforms or Staphylococcus saprophyticus are common causes of urinary tract infections in these cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is true; however, the reason is false as coliforms or Staphylococcus saprophyticus are not implicated when C. trachomatis is isolated.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 5)

items.assertion: Chlamydial conjunctivitis can be differentiated from other forms of neonatal conjunctivitis on clinical grounds.
items.reason: Laboratory diagnosis is required to differentiate chlamydial conjunctivitis from other forms of neonatal conjunctivitis.
items.explanation: It is impossible to differentiate chlamydial conjunctivitis from other forms on clinical grounds; laboratory diagnosis is needed.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.assertion: LGV proctitis in MSM often presents with anorectal pain and mucopurulent, bloody rectal discharge.
items.reason: NAATs for C. trachomatis have led to increased recognition of LGV cases among MSM.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why LGV proctitis presents with these symptoms.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 6)

items.assertion: Urine screening tests are often used in outreach screening programs for chlamydial infections.
items.reason: Urine samples are less invasive and convenient for large-scale screenings.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why urine tests are used.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.assertion: Chlamydial infections can lead to elephantiasis of the genitalia in untreated cases.
items.reason: Lymphatic obstruction from chronic infection causes massive swelling and fibrosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with lymphatic obstruction explaining the development of elephantiasis.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · GENITAL INFECTIONS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 141) (part 7)

items.assertion: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force has named Chlamydia screening as a Grade B recommendation.
items.reason: This means private insurance and Medicare will cover the cost of screening under the Affordable Care Act.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.assertion: Home-based, self-collected testing platforms have reduced the need for clinical settings in chlamydia screening.
items.reason: These platforms emerged after 2018 and expanded STI testing outside of traditional health care settings.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · SEX PARTNERS | NEONATES AND INFANTS

items.assertion: Trachoma is the leading cause of preventable infectious blindness worldwide.
items.reason: C. trachomatis serovars A, B, Ba, and C are isolated from patients with clinical trachoma in endemic areas.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why trachoma is a leading cause of preventable blindness.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.assertion: Active trachoma usually resolves spontaneously by age 10–15.
items.reason: Reinfection contributes to the pathogenesis of trachoma.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but reinfection contributing to pathogenesis does not explain why active trachoma resolves spontaneously.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · TRACHOMA

items.assertion: C. pneumoniae is a common cause of community-acquired pneumonia.
items.reason: Serologic evidence suggests that C. pneumoniae may be associated with up to 10% of cases of community-acquired pneumonia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not fully explain why it is a common cause since most evidence comes from single high IgG titers.
items.reference: Chlamydial Infections · Psittacosis | C. PNEUMONIAE INFECTIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
